turns.content: I thought I just needed to wrap my database error in a custom enum and add an else branch to catch everything, but I keep getting weird output when I try to print the chain. Is that because I forgot about the source field?

That's exactly what happens if you forget it! The source field is required for any variant in your error enum that should represent a wrapped error. Without it, the Display trait can't link the two errors together properly. You need to explicitly declare `#[derive(Debug)]` on your enum and ensure every variant wrapping another error includes `.with(source)` when you create the instance. This allows the compiler to generate the correct debug formatting that shows both the original error and where it occurred.
Ah, so I was just missing the source field entirely? That explains why my error messages were showing two separate errors instead of a chain.

Yes! When you use `thiserror` with `#[from]`, the framework expects that specific field to exist. If it's absent, your code won't compile unless you manually implement Display and Debug yourself, which is error-prone for beginners. The correct pattern uses `thiserror::#derive` macro with `source` as a required attribute on every variant wrapping another error type.
Okay, so I should add the source field to my error variants. But how do I actually use it when propagating errors? Do I just pass the inner error around manually?

You don't need to pass it manually anymore if you configure `thiserror` correctly with `#from` on your custom error type. When that trait is present, the compiler will automatically recognize wrapped errors and convert them into your enum variants using the source field. This means you can just return a downstream error from your function, and it gets automatically mapped to your enum without extra boilerplate code.
That sounds much cleaner than what I was doing before. So `thiserror` handles the conversion automatically as long as I have the right attributes?

Exactly! The `#from` trait acts like a bridge that connects downstream error types to your custom enum variants. Once you define your error type with `source` fields and derive the necessary traits, `thiserror` takes care of the mapping logic for you at compile time.
Perfect. I think I understand now. Thanks for clarifying how source works in this context!
You're welcome! Just remember that source is essential for building readable error chains in Rust when using `thiserror` with enums.

I'm building a proof-of-concept network client and I want to capture exactly which upstream error caused my failure so users can see the full chain. If an HTTP request fails, I need to know if it was DNS or SSL, not just that it failed.

That is a great approach for observability. You should use thiserror to define your error type as an enum and then embed the specific upstream errors using #[from] on their variants so you can automatically propagate them without writing manual matches everywhere.
I think I just need a simple string field in my error struct to store whatever message comes back, maybe with an index. That should be enough to track the source.

That won't work because strings cannot preserve the actual type information of the original error, so you lose the ability to distinguish between a DNS failure and an SSL handshake timeout when displaying the chain. Instead, you should use thiserror's source field which holds the actual upstream error instance itself.
Oh, I see. So if I have a custom HttpError enum with variants for Network and Auth, I just add #[from] to the Network variant so it can unwrap any network crate errors automatically?

Exactly, but you also need to implement Display correctly so that when an error is printed, it shows your custom message followed by 'caused by' and then the details of the embedded source error. Think of it like a Rust chain of custody where each link holds evidence from the previous step.
That analogy makes sense, but I'm worried about the overhead if every single function in my client has to manually call thiserror::#from for every possible error type. Is that really necessary?

No, #[from] is a derive macro that generates the implementation behind the scenes, so you don't write any code for it; you just add the attribute to your variant definition and the compiler handles the rest.

Perfect. So if I use thiserror::#from on my HttpError enum's variants, my application will automatically catch all downstream errors from the network crate without me writing any manual error handling logic.

That is correct. However, you should consider whether you need to distinguish between different types of upstream failures in your display output by adding a custom Display trait implementation if the default behavior isn't specific enough for your needs.

I'm trying to build a deployment validator tool in Rust using thiserror, but my application panics immediately when I run it. The error says "Box<dyn Any>" is not allowed as a generic parameter and the derive macro failed to compile because the trait bounds aren't satisfied.

The issue is that you are trying to use Box<dyn Error> inside your enum variants without properly handling the dynamic dispatch or using the correct type. In thiserror, when you want to embed a source error in an enum variant, you must explicitly mark it with #[from] and ensure the inner type implements the necessary traits. You likely defined something like MyError { cause: Box<dyn Error> } which prevents the derive from working correctly.

Oh I see, I did define my error struct with a Box<dyn Error> inside and then tried to derive thiserror. But even after that it still fails because of the trait bounds on the generic parameter. What exactly is wrong with how I am using #[from]?

The problem is that you need to use a concrete type or ensure the trait bound is satisfied in your enum definition when using #[from]. When embedding source errors, #[from] tells thiserror how to convert an inner error into your outer error. If your variant contains Box<dyn Error>, it won't work because dynamic dispatch breaks the generic constraint required by the derive macro.
So I should just replace Box<dyn Error> with something else? Like a concrete type or maybe use Option instead?

You can either use Option<Error> if you want to allow missing causes, or keep the Box but ensure it contains an actual Error type. However, for most cases with thiserror, you should avoid dynamic dispatch entirely and instead store the source error as a concrete type like std::io::Error or your own custom error type directly in the variant.
Got it. So I change my enum to have variants with concrete types and then use #[from] on those specific fields? Can you show me how to properly structure this?

Yes, that is the correct approach. You define your error enum where each variant holds a specific type like std::io::Error or Result<T, E>, then add #[from] to those fields so that when you catch an inner error, it automatically converts to your outer MyError variant.
Perfect. So I define the enum with concrete types and add #[from] to the cause field, then thiserror will derive the conversion logic for me?

Exactly. Once you structure your error enum that way and apply #[from] to the source field, the derive macro will automatically generate the From trait implementation so your application can propagate errors cleanly without manual conversion code.

I'm trying to build a custom error type for my deployment tool, but when I wrap an existing crate error using the thiserror crate, the display output just shows the original message instead of preserving the context. How do I make it so that the full chain is visible when someone prints the error?

The issue you're facing comes down to how you define your enum and set the source field for each variant. When defining an Enum in thiserror, every error variant needs a source field of type Box<dyn Error + Send> so that it can hold the original error. Then you use #[from] on those variants to tell thiserror that they derive FromError automatically. For example, your DeploymentError enum should look like this: pub enum DeploymentError { ConfigNotFound(ConfigError), Timeout(TimeoutError) } where each variant carries its own source. Without these fields, the Display trait implementation won't know what to show beyond the top-level message.

Okay, so I'm adding those source fields inside each variant like this: ConfigNotFound { source: Box<dyn Error> }, but when I run it, the error still collapses everything into one line. Is there something about how Display or std::error::Error is implemented that I'm missing?

You're missing a key piece: you need to explicitly derive FromError for each variant and ensure your enum derives Debug and Display as well. The #[from] attribute on the variants tells thiserror to implement the From trait for those specific error types, which is crucial for automatic unwrapping. Also, make sure your top-level enum derives both Debug and Display so that when you print the error, it actually iterates through the chain instead of just showing the first element. The Display implementation relies on these traits being present in your enum definition.

I added #[derive(Debug, Display)] to my enum and put #[from] on each variant, but now I'm getting a compile error saying 'thiserror cannot derive Debug for this type'. What's going wrong with my derivation order?

That happens because you're trying to derive multiple traits at once when only some are supported by the macro. In thiserror, you should use #[derive(thiserror::Error, Debug)] on your enum and then add individual #[from] attributes per variant separately. The Error trait from thiserror already includes the necessary logic for chaining sources internally, so you don't need to manually derive Display or Debug on the whole enum if you let thiserror handle it via its own macro. However, if you really want full visibility of the chain in debug output, ensure your Display implementation is correct and that each variant's source field holds a concrete error type rather than just a trait object.

Ah, okay, so I should avoid deriving Debug on the whole enum and let thiserror handle it. But when I print the error now, it still doesn't show the full chain like I expected. Is there another setting or configuration I need to tweak?

The issue is likely that your Display implementation isn't using the correct format string or you're not leveraging the source field properly in your custom Display logic. When using thiserror, the default Display output already includes the chain if you've set up the source fields correctly, but sometimes people forget to use the .source() method in their own Display implementations or they're manually re-implementing Display without calling the standard library's error formatting functions. Try letting the macro-generated code handle everything by removing any manual Display implementation and just relying on #[derive(thiserror::Error)] which will automatically generate the correct output showing the chain.

That makes sense! I removed my custom Display and let thiserror do it, but now the error message looks fine except for one thing: when I try to unwrap an inner error using .source(), it seems to fail because the trait bounds aren't met. How do I ensure that the source errors are actually accessible via the standard Error API?

To make sure your errors work correctly with the standard library's Error API, you need to ensure that each variant in your enum holds a concrete error type rather than just a boxed trait object when possible. While using Box<dyn Error + Send> works for flexibility, it can break some interoperability if not handled carefully. Instead of boxing generic traits, consider defining specific error types like ConfigError and TimeoutError as separate structs or enums, then having your DeploymentError variants wrap those concrete types directly with a source field. This way, when you call .source() on the top-level error, it returns one of these concrete types that implement std::error::Error fully.
